story.title: The Saturday Market
story.description: Zoe is twelve and thinks Saturday mornings are for sleeping. Her grandfather thinks otherwise. Together, they explore the weekly market — and Zoe discovers that knowing how to choose, compare, and care for things is a kind of knowledge worth having.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: a small open shop or table in a market where things are sold
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe walked past the first three stalls because the prices were too high.

vocabulary.definition: to look at two or more things together to find the differences or similarities
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Zoe compared the two apples carefully before choosing the darker one.

vocabulary.definition: one of the foods or substances used to make a dish or recipe
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe checked every ingredient before putting it in his shopping bag.

vocabulary.definition: the taste that a food or drink has
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe said the dark bread had the best flavour in the whole market.

vocabulary.definition: something that is good quality and also sold at a low price
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe found a bargain on tomatoes — three bags for the price of one.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use comparatives to compare two things (bigger, more expensive, better). We use superlatives to say something is the top of a group (the biggest, the most expensive, the best). For short adjectives, add -er and -est. For longer adjectives, use more and most. Some adjectives are irregular: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst.
examples: The darker apple smells stronger and richer than the light one.
The stalls near the entrance are more expensive than the ones in the middle.
This loaf has the best flavour in the whole market.
The walk home was longer than I expected, but the bags were lighter.

content.text: Zoe did not want to go to the market on Saturday morning. She wanted to stay at home. But Grandpa Joe was already at the front door with his shopping bag and hat. "Come on," he said. "I need someone to carry things. You're stronger than me." Zoe looked at him. He was not smaller than her. But she liked him too much to argue. She put on her coat and followed him out.

The market was bigger than Zoe expected. It filled three streets. Stalls sold fruit, vegetables, bread, cheese, flowers, and more. Grandpa Joe walked past the first four stalls. "Those are the most expensive," he said. "The best bargains are never at the entrance." Zoe followed him. The market was louder further in, but the prices on the signs were smaller.

At a stall near the middle, there were apples in four colours. Grandpa Joe picked one up, smelled it, and put it back. Then he picked up a smaller, darker one. "This one's sweeter," he said. "And cheaper." He gave it to Zoe. This one smelled richer and stronger than the light one. "Can I compare them?" she asked. He handed her both. The darker one was clearly better. She chose it.

They moved from stall to stall. Grandpa Joe knew most of the sellers. He compared prices, checked ingredients, and smelled things. At the bread stall, he found a bargain on tomatoes and bought a small dark loaf. "Best flavour in the market," he told the seller. "How do you know which stall is better?" Zoe asked. Grandpa Joe thought about this. "Forty years," he said.

They walked home with two full bags, one heavier than the other. Zoe carried the heavier one. "That took longer than I thought," she said. "Was it more boring than staying at home?" Grandpa Joe asked. Zoe thought about the apple, the bread, and the sellers who knew Grandpa Joe. "No," she said honestly. "It was actually the most interesting Saturday I've had in a while." Grandpa Joe smiled. It was the best kind of smile — the kind that meant he was pleased.

comprehension.question: Why does Grandpa Joe walk past the first four stalls without stopping?
options: He doesn't like the sellers at those stalls.
The stalls near the entrance are the most expensive.
The fruit at those stalls is lower quality than the others.
He already bought things there last week.
comprehension.correctAnswer: The stalls near the entrance are the most expensive.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 2 says: 'Those are the most expensive. The better the location, the higher the price.' Grandpa Joe knows that stalls in the best position charge more, so he goes further in.

comprehension.question: How does Grandpa Joe decide which apple to buy in paragraph 3?
options: He reads the price label and picks the cheapest one.
He asks the seller which apple is the best.
He smells and handles each one, then picks the smaller, darker apple.
He asks Zoe to choose for him.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He smells and handles each one, then picks the smaller, darker apple.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 says Grandpa Joe 'picked one up, smelled it, and put it back.' Then he picked a 'smaller, darker one', saying it was sweeter and cheaper. He uses his senses and experience rather than just the price.

comprehension.question: What does 'compare' mean in paragraph 3?
options: To buy two things at the same time
To smell food before buying it
To look at two things together to find differences
To ask the seller for a lower price
comprehension.correctAnswer: To look at two things together to find differences
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 3, Zoe asks 'Can I compare them?' and then Grandpa Joe gives her both apples. She 'held them side by side' and noticed the difference. This shows that comparing means looking at two things together.

comprehension.question: At the end, Zoe says it was 'the most interesting Saturday she'd had in a while.' What does this tell us about her opinion of the market?
options: She found it interesting but also very tiring.
She changed her mind and now enjoys going to the market.
She is being polite but still thinks it was boring.
She wants to become a market seller one day.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She changed her mind and now enjoys going to the market.
comprehension.explanation: At the start, Zoe did not want to go. She said 'I didn't want to come.' By the end, she uses the superlative 'most interesting' — saying it was better than other recent Saturdays. This shows a genuine change of opinion, not politeness.

vocabulary.definition: a sign of an illness or health problem, such as a pain, fever, or tiredness
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe described his symptoms: an ache in his back and no energy.

vocabulary.definition: to get better after an illness or injury
vocabulary.exampleSentence: By Monday, Grandpa Joe was beginning to recover after resting and drinking water.

vocabulary.definition: the food and drinks that a person usually eats
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Zoe had been reading about healthy diet and wanted to share what she had learned.

vocabulary.definition: having a lot of energy and being active
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe said he felt much more energetic after a good rest and some soup.

vocabulary.definition: a continuous, dull pain in part of your body
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Grandpa Joe had an ache in his back and his legs that would not go away.

grammarFocus.rule: Modal Verbs – can, could, should, must
grammarFocus.explanation: Modal verbs come before the main verb. We use them to talk about ability (can/could), advice (should/shouldn't), or strong necessity (must/mustn't). The main verb stays in its base form after the modal. 'Could' is also used for past ability or polite possibility.
examples: You should eat something, even if it's small. (advice)
You must drink water — it's very important. (strong necessity)
Can you eat soup? — I think I can. (ability: present)
When he was younger, he could walk for hours without stopping. (past ability)

content.text: The following Sunday, Grandpa Joe called Zoe. He said he was not feeling well. His back had an ache, he had no energy, and he didn't want to eat. "You should eat something," Zoe told him. "And you must drink water." She heard him laugh softly. "Since when do you know so much about health?" he asked. "Since last Saturday," she said. "I've been reading about diet." She got on her bicycle before he could say more.

Grandpa Joe was in his armchair when she arrived. He looked pale and tired. "What are your symptoms?" Zoe asked. Grandpa Joe raised his eyebrows. "I ache everywhere," he said. "My back, my legs, my head. I could have a cold, or I could just be getting old." "Could you eat some soup?" Zoe asked. "I think I can," he said.

Zoe made soup from the vegetables they had bought at the market and added the dark bread in small pieces. While it cooked, she sat at the kitchen table and talked. "You should eat more of those dark apples," she told him. "They're better for you." Grandpa Joe looked at her over his reading glasses. "Could you possibly know that for certain?" he asked. "I read it," Zoe said. "You must eat more fruit. It's in the article."

Grandpa Joe's friend Dr Nair came to visit in the afternoon. He was a retired doctor. He looked at Grandpa Joe, listened to his chest, and nodded. "He should rest," he told Zoe. "He must drink plenty of water. He can have soup and soft food, but not too much salt." He looked at Zoe's notes. "Could you be a doctor one day?" he asked. Zoe thought seriously. "Maybe," she said. "I should read more first."

By Monday evening, Grandpa Joe was beginning to recover. He sent Zoe a message: "The soup helped. I feel more energetic today. You were right about the water. Can you come next Saturday? I think we should go to the market again." Zoe read it three times. She felt quiet satisfaction. She had given advice, and it worked. She wrote back: "Yes. I'll carry the bags." Then she went to find the health article. There was a section on diet and exercise she had not read yet.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: should go to the market again

comprehension.question: What advice does Zoe give Grandpa Joe when he calls on Sunday?
options: She tells him to go to the hospital immediately.
She tells him he should eat something and must drink water.
She tells him to rest and not to eat anything.
She tells him to call Dr Nair.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She tells him he should eat something and must drink water.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 says Zoe told him 'You should eat something' and 'you must drink water.' These are two separate pieces of advice — one using 'should' (advice) and one using 'must' (strong necessity).

comprehension.question: What does Grandpa Joe mean when he says 'I could have a cold, or I could just be getting old'?
options: He is certain he has a cold.
He thinks he is too old to get better.
He is suggesting two possible explanations for his symptoms.
He is asking Zoe to help him get a diagnosis.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He is suggesting two possible explanations for his symptoms.
comprehension.explanation: 'Could' in this context expresses possibility — not certainty. He is saying there are two possible reasons for how he feels: illness or age. He doesn't know which one it is.

comprehension.question: What does the word 'symptom' mean in the context of paragraph 2?
options: A medicine you take when you are ill
A sign of an illness such as pain or tiredness
A question a doctor asks a patient
The name of a health condition
comprehension.correctAnswer: A sign of an illness such as pain or tiredness
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 2, Zoe asks 'What are your symptoms?' and Grandpa Joe answers by listing physical problems: his back aches, his legs ache, his head aches. These are the signs that something is wrong — which is what symptoms are.

comprehension.question: What shows that Zoe genuinely enjoyed helping Grandpa Joe, rather than just feeling she had to?
options: She read an article about diet because her teacher told her to.
She felt 'a quiet kind of satisfaction' when her advice worked.
She agreed to go back to the market because she had nothing else to do.
She did not want to leave Grandpa Joe's house.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She felt 'a quiet kind of satisfaction' when her advice worked.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 5 says Zoe 'felt something she hadn't expected — a quiet kind of satisfaction.' This inner feeling shows that she genuinely cared about helping, and that seeing the advice work gave her real pleasure — not just duty.
